title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
research_problem: Identify potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand the vehicle's intended behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior. The study addresses the communication gap between driverless vehicles and pedestrians who traditionally rely on eye contact, hand gestures, and other informal communication with human drivers.
method_summary: VR experiment using Unity 3D and HTC Vive headset. 30 participants completed 16 trials in a 4x4 randomized complete block design testing visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' text, smiley face, no signal) and audible features (horn, music, verbal message, no signal). Data collected via headset tracking (crossing time, waiting time, head rotation) and surveys (feature ratings, PBQ, PRQF, personal innovativeness scale, simulation sickness questionnaire).
metrics: feature_ratings_7point_scale; crossing_time; waiting_time; pedestrian_behavior_questionnaire_pbq; pedestrian_receptivity_questionnaire_prqf; personal_innovativeness_scale; simulation_sickness_questionnaire
claim_text: Pedestrians' receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features (t=2.21, p<0.0349 for comfort; t=4.27, p<0.0002 for acceptance)
limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and always stopping; no other traffic in virtual environment | Proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only; vehicle stopped in every trial | First exposure to FAV for all participants; learning curve effects not fully controlled | Limited sample size (n=30) with narrow age range (18-47) | Non-signalized intersection only; did not test signalized crossings
notes: Study validated VR platform for pedestrian safety research. Average walking speed in VR (1.54 m/sec) was faster than general walking speed (1.22 m/sec), possibly due to uncertainty about driverless vehicle. VR allowed participants to experience interaction with FAV before market deployment. Feature ratings used 7-point scale where higher scores indicate greater preference. Visual features had stronger effect size (g2=0.151) than audible features (g2=0.086) on ratings.

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Pedestrians' Red-light running behavior is one of the most critical factors for pedestrian involved traffic crashes at intersections in China. The study aims to explore how various factors affect pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersection areas, using data collected from Hefei, China.
method_summary: A questionnaire survey (SP survey) with 15 items was designed to collect pedestrians' socio-economic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples collected from six signalized intersections in Hefei, a binomial logistic regression model was established using Score test for variable selection and iterative calculation to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrians' red-light running behavior.
datasets: 631 valid survey samples from Hefei, China (collected May-June 2015); Six typical signalized intersections selected near central business district or transit stations
metrics: Score test (significance testing); Odds ratio; p-values; 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Four variables significantly affect the probability of pedestrians' red-light running behavior: trip purpose, time period in a day, pedestrian's attitude towards running a red light when in hurry, and pedestrian's attitude towards whether quality of road facility affects crossing behavior.
limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei), so results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to differences in culture and driving behaviors | Sample size of 631 may be insufficient for comprehensive validation; more data from multiple intersections in other cities should be collected | Study only considered pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersection areas, ignoring other types of crossing facilities (e.g., midblock crossings with/without crosswalk buttons) | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors while ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and herd mentality effects in group behavior analysis
notes: The study uses binomial logistic regression with dummy variable encoding for categorical variables. The final model identifies four significant predictor variables out of 15 explanatory variables initially considered. The probability prediction function is provided in Equation 3 of the paper. The study addresses a significant public health issue in China where pedestrian traffic crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths.

notes: The study uses binomial logistic regression with dummy variable encoding for categorical variables. The final model identifies four significant predictor variables out of 15 explanatory variables initially considered. The probability prediction function is provided in Equation 3 of the paper. The study addresses a significant public health issue in China where pedestrian traffic crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths.

The study addresses a significant public health issue in China where pedestrian traffic crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths.

metrics: Score test (significance testing); Odds ratio; p-values; 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Pedestrians whose crossing behavior is affected by road facility quality are more likely to run red lights (odds ratio = 0.483 for unaffected group)
limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei), so results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to differences in culture and driving behaviors | Sample size of 631 may be insufficient for comprehensive validation; more data from multiple intersections in other cities should be collected | Study only considered pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersection areas, ignoring other types of crossing facilities (e.g., midblock crossings with/without crosswalk buttons) | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors while ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and herd mentality effects in group behavior analysis

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: Identify factors affecting pedestrian red light running propensity at signalized intersections in urban Hong Kong, as red light running violation is a leading cause of pedestrian crashes at these locations with high crash involvement rates
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong, capturing crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during 652 signal cycles. A random parameter logit model was developed to examine the association between red light running propensity and both individual-level (personal demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity
method_family: Random parameter logit model (discrete choice model)
tasks: Analyze pedestrian red light running behavior; Identify effects of personal factors (gender, age, companion presence); Identify effects of environmental factors (signal time, traffic condition); Examine interaction effects between personal and environmental factors
datasets: Video observation data from 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong (6320 pedestrians, 652 cycles)
metrics: Red light running violation (binary outcome); Odds ratios; Marginal effects/elasticities; AIC for model comparison
claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio 0.667), with 64.3% probability of lower propensity
limitations: Study focuses on red light running behavior but does not assess safety outcomes or associated crash risk | Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior was not measured despite its correlation with crash risk and severity | Limited to six sites in urban Hong Kong, may not generalize to other contexts with different pedestrian signal systems (Hong Kong lacks countdown devices)
notes: First study to jointly examine individual-level and cycle-level factors on pedestrian red light running decision; Novel examination of social influences including presence of companion, number of pedestrians around, and violation behavior of others; Random parameter approach justified by significant standard deviation of gender effect (1.163), indicating unobserved heterogeneity among female pedestrians; 18.5% of observed pedestrians (1169 out of 6320) committed red light running violations; Study locations were pedestrian crash hot spots with >10 pedestrian crashes in preceding 5 years

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: Identify factors affecting pedestrian red light running propensity at signalized intersections in urban Hong Kong, as red light running violation is a leading cause of pedestrian crashes at these locations with high crash involvement rates

claim_text: Presence of other pedestrians violating red light significantly increases individual red light running propensity (odds ratio 4.023), demonstrating vicarious experience of punishment avoidance
limitations: Study focuses on red light running behavior but does not assess safety outcomes or associated crash risk | Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior was not measured despite its correlation with crash risk and severity | Limited to six sites in urban Hong Kong, may not generalize to other contexts with different pedestrian signal systems (Hong Kong lacks countdown devices)
notes: First study to jointly examine individual-level and cycle-level factors on pedestrian red light running decision; Novel examination of social influences including presence of companion, number of pedestrians around, and violation behavior of others; Random parameter approach justified by significant standard deviation of gender effect (1.163), indicating unobserved heterogeneity among female pedestrians; 18.5% of observed pedestrians (1169 out of 6320) committed red light running violations; Study locations were pedestrian crash hot spots with >10 pedestrian crashes in preceding 5 years

authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: Identify factors affecting pedestrian red light running propensity at signalized intersections in urban Hong Kong, as red light running violation is a leading cause of pedestrian crashes at these locations with high crash involvement rates
